3	# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
4	# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
5	#
6	# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
7	# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
8	#
9	# Fields:
10	#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
11	#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
12	#   codex             — target codex name
13	#   complexity        — patron estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
14	#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
15	#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
16	#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
17	#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
18	#   reviewed_at       — ISO date when patron reviewed this commission (null = not reviewed)
19	#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
20	#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
21	#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
22	#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
23	#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
24	#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
25	#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
26	#
27	# Scales:
28	#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
29	#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
30	#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
31	#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.

# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
#

#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
#   codex             — target codex name
#   complexity        — patron estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review

#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
#
# Scales:
#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

# Brief: Kit-Contributed Rig Templates, Writ Types, and Mappings

## Motivation

Today, adding a new writ type with its own execution pipeline requires the guild owner to manually configure three things in `guild.json`:

1. Register the writ type with the Clerk (`clerk.writTypes`)
2. Define a rig template for it (`spider.rigTemplates`)
3. Map the writ type to the template (`spider.rigTemplates` key)

This means a plugin that wants to introduce a new kind of work — say, a `quality-audit` writ with its own multi-engine pipeline — can ship all the engine designs and block types it needs via kit contributions, but *cannot* wire them up without the guild owner editing config. The last mile is manual.

The goal is **plug-and-play work types**: a kit or apparatus can contribute a writ type, a rig template, and the mapping between them, so that installing the plugin is sufficient to make the new work type functional. Guild config retains override authority for any of these contributions.

## Key Features

### 1. Kit-contributed rig templates

Kits may contribute named rig templates via a `rigTemplates` field (a `Record<string, RigTemplate>`). The Spider consumes these, merging them into its template registry alongside config-defined templates.

**Follow the `roles` pattern from the Loom** for scoping, naming, and override semantics:

- Kit-contributed templates are namespace-qualified: `pluginId.templateName` (e.g., `quality-tools.audit`).
- Guild config templates (in `spider.rigTemplates`) are unqualified names.
- Guild config overrides kit contributions: if `spider.rigTemplates` defines a key matching a kit's qualified name, the config wins and the kit contribution is silently skipped.
- Dependency-scoped validation: a kit's rig template may only reference engine designIds from plugins declared in its `requires` or `recommends`, plus the Spider's own built-in engines.

The Spider declares `consumes: ['rigTemplates']` and scans kits at startup + subscribes to `plugin:initialized`, same as it does for `blockTypes`.

### 2. Kit-contributed writ types

Kits may contribute a `writTypes` field (an array of writ type descriptors, same shape as `clerk.writTypes` config entries). The Clerk consumes these, merging them with config-defined writ types.

- Kit-contributed writ type names are **not** namespace-qualified — they are user-facing names that appear on writs (e.g., `quality-audit`, not `quality-tools.quality-audit`). A kit contributing a writ type is asserting "this name should exist in this guild."
- Config-defined writ types override kit contributions with the same name.
- The Clerk declares `consumes: ['writTypes']`.

### 3. Kit-contributed rig template mappings

Kits may contribute a `rigTemplateMappings` field (a `Record<string, string>` mapping writ type → rig template name). The Spider consumes these.

- Values may reference any registered rig template name, including qualified kit-contributed names (e.g., `quality-tools.audit`).
- Guild config mappings override kit contributions for the same writ type.
- The Spider declares `consumes: ['rigTemplateMappings']` (or folds this into its existing `rigTemplates` consumption — planner's call on the cleanest approach).

### 4. Lookup changes

`lookupTemplate()` currently does a direct property lookup on `config.rigTemplates`. After this work, it needs to consult the merged registry: config-defined templates + kit-contributed templates + config mappings + kit-contributed mappings, with config winning on conflicts at every layer.

The `'default'` fallback template continues to work as today.

## The plug-and-play story

A plugin shipping all three contributions looks like:

```ts
export const plugin = {
  kit: {
    requires: ['spider', 'clerk'],
    // 1. New writ type
    writTypes: [{ name: 'quality-audit', description: 'Automated quality audit' }],
    // 2. Rig template for it
    rigTemplates: {
      audit: { engines: [/* ... */] },
    },
    // 3. Wire them together
    rigTemplateMappings: {
      'quality-audit': 'quality-tools.audit',
    },
  },
};
```

Installing this plugin is sufficient. No `guild.json` changes required. The guild owner *can* override any piece (swap in a different template, redefine the writ type, remap to a different template) via config, but doesn't have to.

## Out of scope

- Changes to engine design contribution (already works via Fabricator kits)
- Changes to block type contribution (already works)
- Any new writ lifecycle behavior — this is purely about type registration and rig template wiring
- Rig template inheritance or composition (templates are atomic)

## Validation

- Startup validation should cover the merged registries: duplicate detection, dangling references (mapping points to nonexistent template, template references nonexistent engine design), cycle detection in engine DAGs.
- Validation errors from kit contributions should identify the contributing kit in the error message (as the Loom does for role validation).

content.text:
There it is! 🎯

The reader session hit a **permissions wall** — it requested read access to `/workspace/nexus-mk2` and never got it granted. Look at those first two tool results:

> *"Claude requested permissions to read from /workspace/nexus-mk2, but you haven't granted it yet."*

The session ran headless (no one to click "Allow"), so every read attempt returned that permission-denied message instead of actual file content. The reader was flying blind — it couldn't read any source files from the codebase. The "File created successfully" for `inventory.md` at the end is suspicious too; if it was using a relative path (`specs/kit-contributed-rig-templates-writ/inventory.md`) without the `/workspace/nexus-mk2` prefix, the write might have been working from a different CWD, or the file got created somewhere transient.

**Root cause:** The reader agent session doesn't have pre-granted filesystem permissions for `/workspace/nexus-mk2`. Other spec directories' readers presumably ran with proper permissions (or the permission model changed since then).

**Question for you, Sean:** How are reader sessions launched? Is there a permission allowlist or `--allowedTools` config that needs updating for this directory? Or was there a one-time permission grant that expired/wasn't set up for this run?

Here is the brief:

# Brief: Kit-Contributed Rig Templates, Writ Types, and Mappings

## Motivation

Today, adding a new writ type with its own execution pipeline requires the guild owner to manually configure three things in `guild.json`:

1. Register the writ type with the Clerk (`clerk.writTypes`)
2. Define a rig template for it (`spider.rigTemplates`)
3. Map the writ type to the template (`spider.rigTemplates` key)

This means a plugin that wants to introduce a new kind of work — say, a `quality-audit` writ with its own multi-engine pipeline — can ship all the engine designs and block types it needs via kit contributions, but *cannot* wire them up without the guild owner editing config. The last mile is manual.

The goal is **plug-and-play work types**: a kit or apparatus can contribute a writ type, a rig template, and the mapping between them, so that installing the plugin is sufficient to make the new work type functional. Guild config retains override authority for any of these contributions.

content.text:
OK, I've got the full picture now. Here's what happened, Sean:

## Diagnosis

**Two problems, one root cause.**

### Problem 1: Permission denials on reads
The reader's first few tool calls tried to Glob and Read from `/workspace/nexus-mk2` — the sanctum path. It got 4 permission denials:

```
Claude requested permissions to read from /workspace/nexus-mk2, but you haven't granted it yet.
```

The `--add-dir` flag adds `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs` (SPECS_DIR), and the CWD is the temp clone. Neither grants read access to `/workspace/nexus-mk2` itself. The agent recovered by switching to relative paths from the CWD (the temp clone), so it *did* successfully read the codebase and produce an inventory.

### Problem 2: Inventory written to wrong location (the actual failure)
The prompt says: `create an inventory at: /workspace/nexus-mk2/specs/kit-contributed-rig-templates-writ/inventory.md` (absolute path). But the agent wrote to `specs/kit-contributed-rig-templates-writ/inventory.md` — a **relative path**. Since the CWD is the temp clone (`/tmp/plan-nexus-<uuid>/`), the file landed inside the temp dir, not in the specs directory.

Then the pipeline's post-reader check looks for the inventory at the absolute specs path, doesn't find it, and logs: *"Reader finished but no inventory.md produced."*

### Why is this new?
The reader agent is running Sonnet (`'sonnet'` is passed to `runPipelineStep`). Looking at the tool calls, it started with absolute paths (`/workspace/nexus-mk2/...`), got permission-denied, then overcorrected to relative paths for *everything* — including the output Write. Previous runs likely didn't hit the permission denial on the first Glob attempts, so the agent stayed in absolute-path mode throughout.

The `--add-dir SPECS_DIR` grants access to `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs`, but the agent's *first* Glob was to `/workspace/nexus-mk2` (the parent), which is **not** covered. Once the agent learns "that path is off-limits," it avoids absolute paths entirely — including for the Write to specs.

### Fix options

1. **Add `--add-dir /workspace/nexus-mk2`** (or PROJECT_ROOT) to the CLI args — grants read access to the whole sanctum, not just specs. Broadest fix, might be more access than intended.

2. **Change `--permission-mode` to `bypassPermissions`** — skips all permission prompts. Nuclear option; removes the safety net.

3. **Add both dirs**: keep `--add-dir SPECS_DIR` and add `--add-dir PROJECT_ROOT` — but the reader shouldn't need sanctum access, it only needs the codex clone + specs output dir.

4. **The targeted fix** — the real issue is the agent using a relative path for the Write despite being given an absolute one. The prompt already says the absolute path. We could reinforce it, but that's fragile. More reliably: since `--add-dir SPECS_DIR` should grant *write* access to specs with `acceptEdits`, the issue is specifically that the agent chose a relative path after getting spooked by permission denials on `/workspace/nexus-mk2`. **If we stop the agent from ever trying `/workspace/nexus-mk2`**, the permission denial never happens and it doesn't overcorrect. The `--add-dir` for specs is the trigger — it leaks the `/workspace/nexus-mk2` path into the agent's awareness.

My **recommendation**: the simplest reliable fix is option **(1)** — add `PROJECT_ROOT` to the allowed dirs. The reader already gets the specs path in its prompt, so it knows about the sanctum anyway. This prevents the permission-denial → relative-path cascade. Want me to make that change?

content.text:
Nope — the brief doesn't mention `/workspace/nexus-mk2` anywhere. It's all relative references ("guild.json", "spider.rigTemplates", etc.).

The leak is `--add-dir SPECS_DIR`. When Claude launches with `--add-dir /workspace/nexus-mk2/specs`, that path shows up in the session context — the agent *sees* `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs` and infers there's a project at `/workspace/nexus-mk2`. So it Globs the parent to orient itself.

The prompt says "the codebase is in your current working directory," but the `--add-dir` path is a stronger signal — it's a concrete absolute path the agent can explore. First instinct: go look at what's there.

Previous readers probably did the same thing, but either (a) got lucky and the permission prompt was accepted, or (b) the `--add-dir` path was less conspicuous in older setups. The CWD fix commit (`52530a3`) added the explicit "codebase is in your current working directory" hint specifically because "Sonnet occasionally tried to explore /workspace/ instead of the temp clone cwd" — same underlying instinct, same root cause.

So the options narrow a bit:

1. **Obscure the sanctum path** — pass a symlink or relative `--add-dir` so the agent doesn't see `/workspace/nexus-mk2`. Hacky.
2. **Grant read on PROJECT_ROOT** — accept that the agent will sniff around the sanctum, just make sure it doesn't blow up when it does.
3. **Don't use `--add-dir` at all** — write specs into the temp clone dir, then copy them out after the session ends. The pipeline already controls pre/post, so this is mechanical. Eliminates the path leak entirely.

Option 3 is the cleanest — it removes the information leak rather than patching around it. The pipeline would write the brief into the temp clone before launch, and copy inventory.md out after the reader finishes. No `--add-dir`, no sanctum paths visible to the agent at all. Thoughts?

message.content: we talked about 3 in the past, and decided it was a bit janky... but maybe its worth it here

content.text:
Yeah, I remember seeing that in the session notes — the jankiness was the file-shuffling between steps since the clone gets nuked and re-created each time. Let me lay out what it actually looks like concretely:

**Per-step file shuttle:**

| Step | Copy IN (before launch) | Copy OUT (after exit) |
|---|---|---|
| Reader | `brief.md` | `inventory.md` |
| Analyst | `brief.md`, `inventory.md` | `scope.yaml`, `decisions.yaml`, `observations.md` |
| Writer | `inventory.md`, `scope.yaml`, `decisions.yaml`, `decisions-digest.yaml` | `spec.md`, `gaps.yaml` |

That's 3-4 files per step. Not horrific. Could define it declaratively — each step declares its inputs and outputs, the pipeline copies in/out mechanically. The agent prompt switches from absolute paths to something like `_planning/inventory.md` relative to CWD, and `--add-dir` goes away entirely.

lines: item: "Review w-mnorf3k0 (Oculus), run instruments, add nsg oculus startup tool (in nexus), add X013 dashboard-vs-dashboard observation"
+    item: "Fix plan workshop reader failure — replace --add-dir with _planning/ file shuttle to eliminate sanctum path leak"

input.command:
git add bin/plan-review.ts experiments/data/coco-log.yaml && GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
plan workshop: replace --add-dir with _planning/ file shuttle

The --add-dir SPECS_DIR flag leaked the sanctum path (/workspace/nexus-mk2)
into the agent's awareness. The agent would Glob the parent path, hit
permission denials, then overcorrect to relative paths for everything —
including the output Write, which landed in the temp clone instead of the
specs dir. The inventory.md vanished when the clone was cleaned up.

Fix: remove --add-dir entirely. Each pipeline step now declares its
input/output files. The pipeline copies inputs into a _planning/ dir
inside the temp clone before launch, and copies outputs back to the
specs dir after the session ends. The agent never sees sanctum paths.

Session: 4028f87b-f6d0-4289-bfb3-5039e4187e1f
EOF
)"

content.text:
Done! 🐒

**Summary of what changed:**

- **Removed** `--add-dir SPECS_DIR` from the Claude CLI args — no more sanctum paths visible to the agent
- **Added** `STEP_FILES` declaration mapping each step to its input/output files
- **Added** `copyPlanningInputs()` — runs before agent launch, stages files into `_planning/` inside the temp clone
- **Added** `copyPlanningOutputs()` — runs after agent exit (before clone cleanup), copies results back to the specs dir
- **Updated** all step prompts (reader, analyst, analyst-revise, writer) to reference `_planning/` relative paths instead of absolute sanctum paths

The agent now sees a clean world: codex source at CWD, planning artifacts at `_planning/`, nothing else. Want to re-run the reader for that kit-contributed-rig-templates spec to test it?
